hints.Ego: 'Ego' is a personal pronoun meaning 'I' — the subject
hints.vos: 'vos' is a personal pronoun meaning 'you (plural)' — the direct object
hints.aqua: 'aqua' is a noun meaning 'water' in the ablative

hints.to immerse: 'to immerse' is 'immergere' — 'baptizo' comes from 'baptizare' (a Greek loanword)

hints.Imperfect: Imperfect would be 'baptizabam' — 'baptizo' with -o ending is 1st singular present
hints.Future: Future would be 'baptizabo' — 'baptizo' is present indicative

hints.lavo, lavare, lavi, lautum: These are the parts of 'lavare' (to wash)
hints.purgo, purgare, purgavi, purgatum: These are the parts of 'purgare' (to cleanse/purge)
hints.mergo, mergere, mersi, mersum: These are the parts of 'mergere' (to plunge/dip)

hints.Nominative: 'aqua' could be nominative, but here it is ablative of means (baptize with water)
hints.Vocative: Vocative would be the same form, but 'aqua' is ablative of means here
hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'aquam' — 'aqua' is ablative (with water)

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'aquae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'aquae' ends in -ae
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'aquae' ends in -ae

steps.prompt: Why is 'Ego' expressed when 'baptizo' already implies 'I'?

hints.It is grammatically required: Latin verb endings already encode the subject — 'Ego' is not required but emphatic
hints.To indicate 1st person: The -o ending of 'baptizo' already shows 1st person — 'Ego' adds emphasis
hints.It is a scribal addition: 'Ego' is original — it creates contrast (I baptize with water, but HE...)
